stories.title: Equus Acquires Class A Industrial Duo in Supply-Constrained Boston Submarket
stories.excerpt: An affiliate of Equus Capital Partners, Ltd. has acquired the 300,075-square-foot Centech Park North in Shrewsbury MA for an undisclosed sum. The acquisition was completed through a programmatic joint venture. Developed by NorthBridge Partners and completed in 2023, Centech Park North is comprised of two Class A industrial properties. The park is currently 100% leased to a diverse tenant roster spanning the life sciences, advanced manufacturing and commercial services sectors. “Centech Park North represents a compelling opportunity to acquire a recently developed Class A industrial park in one of Greater Boston’s most supply-constrained and fundamentally strong industrial submarkets,” said Equus SVP Keith Hontz, who oversaw the acquisition with analyst Shane Mullen..He added that the park “aligns exceptionally well with our investment strategy of acquiring high-quality industrial assets positioned for long-term growth with the flexibility to accommodate large and small users alike given its shallow-bay configuration and ease of division.” The post Equus Acquires Class A Industrial Duo in Supply-Constrained Boston Submarket appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Equus Capital acquired 300k-sf Centech Park North in Shrewsbury MA, 100% leased, 2023-built.
Life sciences and advanced manufacturing tenants drive demand in Boston's constrained industrial submarkets.
Shallow-bay, divisible configuration signals continued need for flex industrial space near biotech cluster.
stories.summary: Equus Capital Partners acquired the recently completed Centech Park North, a 300,075-sf Class A industrial park in Shrewsbury, MA, through a joint venture. The asset is fully leased to tenants in life sciences, advanced manufacturing, and commercial services. The deal underscores sustained demand for modern industrial facilities in one of Greater Boston's tightest industrial submarkets, where supply constraints support investor confidence in long-term growth.
stories.impact: Life sciences and biotech expansion in Boston metro is absorbing new industrial supply faster than it arrives. Shallow-bay, divisible buildings with flex configurations remain scarce; developers should prioritize similar specs in this submarket—cap rates remain attractive despite tight vacancy and supply constraints will support above-market rent growth for years.

stories.title: Cox Castle Advises on IPX Power’s $4.95B Darden Project
stories.excerpt: Cox, Castle & Nicholson LLP served as real estate and permitting counsel to IPX Power, LLC on the committed financing for the construction and operation of its Darden renewable energy projects in Fresno County, California.  The financing encompasses $4.95 billion of construction debt. It includes a $403-million letter of credit facility, a $911-million tax equity bridge loan, a $1.81-billion tax credit transfer bridge loan, and a $1.83-billion construction loan that will convert to a $1.83-billion term loan upon project completion, an aggregate $929 million of tax equity commitments, and tax credit purchase agreements for Darden’s aggregate $2.13-billion investment tax credits. The Cox Castle real estate team was led by partners Dan Engler and Adriana Vesci with essential support from associates Chris Valentino, Max Liaw, and Kevin Dousa. The Cox Castle permitting team was led by partners Annie Mudge and Robbie Hull with support from partners Rachel Jones and Peter Weiner.   Along with serving as counsel on the land acquisition financing, Cox Castle acted as lead real estate and permitting counsel for the project, advising on efforts to secure site control for the project and obtain approvals under California Energy Commission’s AB 205 opt-in certification and other agency regulations.  “Darden represents one of the most significant and complex renewable energy projects in the country, and we are proud to have supported IPX Power on both the development and financing efforts that have been critical to moving the project forward,” said Engler.  Darden is the first project financing for IPX Power since its formation as an independent power producer earlier this year. Located on privately owned retired agricultural land in the Central Valley, Darden is expected to generate up to 1.15 GWac / 1.6 GWp of solar power and will include 4.6 GWh of battery storage. It’s expe
bullets: IPX Power secures $4.95B construction debt for Darden renewable energy projects in Fresno County CA.
Financing includes $1.83B construction loan, $911M tax equity bridge, $2.13B in tax credits.
Complex permitting and land acquisition point to multi-year buildout in Fresno County region.
stories.summary: IPX Power, a newly formed independent power producer, closed $4.95 billion in committed construction financing for the Darden renewable energy complex in Fresno County, California. The financing structure combines $1.83 billion in construction debt converting to term, $911 million in tax equity bridges, and $2.13 billion in transferable investment tax credits. The project required extensive permitting and real estate counsel for site control and California Energy Commission approvals.
stories.impact: Large-scale renewable energy projects draw institutional capital and create long-term workforce demand in inland California. Fresno County will see sustained construction employment and eventual operating jobs; developers should monitor housing and workforce housing needs in Fresno/Kern region as Darden ramps—this could signal demand for modular/affordable rental supply near energy and industrial facilities.

stories.title: EQT Snags Three-Building Inland Empire East Portfolio
stories.excerpt: Cushman & Wakefield arranged the sale of a three-building industrial portfolio totaling approximately 667,024 square feet (sf) at Interchange Logistics Center in San Bernardino. Jeff Chiate, Rick Ellison, Matt Leupold and Aubrie Monahan represented both the seller, Cabot Properties, and the buyer, EQT. Terms weren’t disclosed. “This portfolio represented a rare opportunity to achieve immediate scale in the Inland Empire East submarket through the acquisition of three institutional-quality industrial assets,” said Chiate. “The project’s high-quality construction, strategic location and robust tenant occupancy underscore the continued demand for modern logistics facilities in one of the nation’s premier industrial and distribution corridors.” The portfolio, located at 3392 & 3524 Mike Daley Dr. and 1651 Interchange Dr., is situated on nearly 35 acres and features building sizes ranging from 94,108 to 354,108 square feet. The assets benefit from immediate access to major transportation arteries, including regional highways and Interstates. The post EQT Snags Three-Building Inland Empire East Portfolio appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: EQT acquires 667k-sf three-building portfolio at Interchange Logistics Center, San Bernardino CA.
35-acre site with building sizes 94k–354k sf, high occupancy, immediate highway/I-15 access.
Institutional quality and logistics corridor proximity drive continued capital inflow to Inland Empire East.
stories.summary: EQT purchased a three-building, 667,024-sf industrial portfolio at Interchange Logistics Center in San Bernardino for undisclosed terms, representing rare infill-scale acquisition in the Inland Empire East. The portfolio spans 35 acres with buildings ranging from 94k to 354k sf, boasts strong occupancy, and offers direct access to major transportation corridors including Interstate 15. Brokers highlighted immediate logistics demand and institutional-quality construction.
stories.impact: Inland Empire East remains a primary institutional capital target for industrial, signaling continued logistics demand from e-commerce and last-mile networks serving Southern California. Modern infill industrial is scarce; developers with entitlements for 50k–250k-sf buildings in South King County, Riverside, or San Bernardino have favorable risk-adjusted returns ahead of further cap-rate compression.

stories.title: BKM Capital Partners Acquires Kent Valley Light Industrial Portfolio
stories.excerpt: BKM Capital Partners has acquired a five-building Metro Seattle industrial portfolio. The assets, located in the market’s premier South King County industrial corridor, were acquired on behalf of BKM Industrial Value Fund III. Totaling 401,000 square feet across approximately 19 acres of infill industrial space, the portfolio is 84% occupied. “The portfolio offers several features that are difficult to replicate in infill locations, like flexible suite sizes, low office finish, and direct access to the region’s core transportation network,” said Brett Turner, Senior Managing Director of Acquisitions and Dispositions with BKM. Turner led the acquisition process for BKM, with support from Michael Grossner, Senior Director of Acquisitions and Dispositions. Buzz Ellis, Managing Director with JLL Capital Markets, represented the seller in the transaction. BKM has allocated approximately $4 million for capital improvements across the portfolio. The acquisition further strengthens BKM’s footprint in the Kent Valley, one of the Pacific Northwest’s most important industrial corridors and a central distribution hub for users serving Seattle, Bellevue, Tacoma, the Port of Seattle, the Port of Tacoma, and Seattle-Tacoma International Airport. Join the Executives Driving Western Industrial Real Estate | August 20Hear from senior leaders representing the industry’s most active firms as they discuss investment strategy, market fundamentals, leasing trends, and emerging opportunities across the West Coast. Connect Industrial West is where industry leaders come together to share insights, build relationships, and identify what’s next for the sector. Secure your spot: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post BKM Capital Partners Acquires Kent Valley Light Industrial Portfolio appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: BKM Capital acquires five-building, 401k-sf portfolio in South King County WA, 84% occupied.
Infill site near SeaTac airport, Port of Seattle/Tacoma, with flexible suites and direct freeway access.
BKM allocates $4M in capex; strengthens position in Kent Valley industrial corridor.
stories.summary: BKM Capital Partners acquired a five-building, 401,000-sf light industrial portfolio in South King County (Kent Valley), Seattle metro, for an undisclosed sum on behalf of BKM Industrial Value Fund III. The 84%-occupied portfolio sits on ~19 acres of infill land with flexible suite sizes, low office finish, and direct access to Interstate 5 and regional distribution hubs. BKM is investing $4 million in capital improvements and reinforcing its foothold in this critical Pacific Northwest distribution corridor.
stories.impact: Kent Valley (South King County) is an irreplaceable infill industrial submarket serving Seattle, Tacoma, and both regional ports. Limited land availability and distribution-hub status support rent growth; developers should prioritize infill redevelopment opportunities near I-5 interchange—low office finish and flex sizing remain currency in this corridor as 3PL and final-mile operators expand in the PNW.

stories.title: Bridges Development Group Buys Kingswood Center in Brooklyn for $31M
stories.excerpt: Kingswood Center in Brooklyn’s Midwood neighborhood has a new owner, Commercial Observer has learned. The 229,926-square-foot shopping center at 1630 East 15th Street sold for $31 million to Michael Berfield’s Bridges Development Group, according to broker JLL. The sale marks a steep discount from the $89 million that the shopping center fetched in early 2020, the last time it traded. The three-story, Class B property is currently anchored by T.J. Maxx. A LoopNet listing advertises nearly 100,000 square feet of retail and commercial space available for lease. Kingswood Center was constructed in 2007 and renovated in 2019, according to the listing, and features an on-site parking garage. JLL’s Ethan Stanton, Michael Mazzara and Brendan Maddigan represented the seller, PNC’s Midland Loan Services, in direct negotiations with the buyer. A JLL debt team of Scott Aiese and Alex Staikos secured $34.65 million in acquisition financing for the buyer. The firm declined to disclose the lender. The financing will allow Bridges Development Group to execute a business plan of investing in the property and leasing its vacant space, according to a JLL spokesperson. Midland took possession of the property from its former owner, retail shopping REIT Urban Edge Properties, in 2023. The REIT fell behind on a $66 million commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) loan used to acquire the center in February 2020. Following the ill-timed purchase, the shopping center’s annual net operating income fell to $1.9 million and occupancy declined to 35 percent, The Real Deal reported at the time. Urban Edge Properties still owns the nearby Kingswood Crossing at 1715 East 13th Street, according to its website. Spokespeople for Bridges Development Group and PNC did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Bridges Development Group acquired Kingswood Center Brooklyn (229k sf) for $31M, down 65% from $89M in 2020.
T.J. Maxx-anchored, Class B with ~100k sf available for lease; JLL secured $34.65M acquisition financing.
Deep discount reflects retail distress; affordable retail and mixed-use conversion opportunity in Midwood.
stories.summary: Bridges Development Group purchased Kingswood Center, a 229,926-sf shopping center in Brooklyn's Midwood neighborhood, for $31 million—a 65% discount from its $89 million sale in early 2020. The three-story, Class B property is anchored by T.J. Maxx and offers ~100k sf of available retail/commercial space. JLL arranged $34.65 million in acquisition financing, enabling conversion and repositioning of the distressed asset.
stories.impact: Retail-anchored properties trading at steep discounts create conversion opportunities for adaptive-use developers; Midwood's dense residential base and underutilized retail footprint suggest mixed-use (retail + multifamily conversion) feasibility. Monitor similar shopping-center distress in outer-borough NYC for acquisition-plus-conversion plays targeting workforce and middle-income renters.

stories.title: First Washington Realty Expands PNW Presence, Acquires Evergreen Village
stories.excerpt: First Washington Realty (FWR) has acquired Evergreen Village, a 123,562-square-foot grocery-anchored community shopping center in Bellevue, Washington, expanding the firm’s Pacific Northwest portfolio to 2.3 million square feet. Anchored by a Safeway supermarket, Evergreen Village features a diverse mix of fitness and dining tenants, including Edgeworks Climbing, Puetz Golf Superstore, and Starbucks. “Evergreen Village is exactly the kind of property we look for,” said Sarah Ellis, Managing Director, Investments for FWR. “It’s a grocery-anchored center in a well-established neighborhood, and Safeway has been serving the Bellevue community for over 50 years.” Positioned in one of the Pacific Northwest’s most established retail corridors, in the heart of Bellevue’s high-growth Eastside, the center serves a dense population within a rapidly expanding submarket. Evergreen Village is situated near major local employers, including Microsoft, Amazon, Meta, and T-Mobile. FWR is a vertically integrated, institutional real estate investment firm with a national portfolio of 138 open-air properties totaling more than 21.5 million square feet across 21 states and Washington, D.C. The post First Washington Realty Expands PNW Presence, Acquires Evergreen Village appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: First Washington Realty acquired Evergreen Village, 123k-sf grocery-anchored center in Bellevue WA.
Safeway anchor, diverse tenants (Edgeworks, Puetz, Starbucks); near Microsoft, Amazon, Meta, T-Mobile HQs.
FWR now holds 2.3M sf across PNW; stable grocery-anchored retail in high-growth tech corridor.
stories.summary: First Washington Realty acquired Evergreen Village, a 123,562-sf grocery-anchored community shopping center in Bellevue, Washington, expanding its Pacific Northwest portfolio to 2.3 million square feet. The property is anchored by a Safeway supermarket and features a mix of fitness and dining tenants. The Bellevue Eastside location is proximate to major tech employers including Microsoft, Amazon, Meta, and T-Mobile, positioning it in a rapidly expanding, high-density submarket.
stories.impact: Bellevue/Eastside tech corridor (Microsoft, Amazon, Meta campuses) continues absorbing workforce and population. Grocery-anchored retail near major employment centers signals sustained neighborhood formation and middle-income housing demand; developers should focus on transit-proximate, mixed-use residential (workforce housing, missing middle) near Bellevue tech cluster—retail anchors stabilize ground-floor economics.

stories.title: Data Intelligence Firm Curinos Takes 14K SF at Marx Realty’s 10 Grand Central
stories.excerpt: Curinos, a global data intelligence firm serving the financial industry, is relocating to Marx Realty’s 10 Grand Central after signing a 14,000-square-foot lease, Commercial Observer has learned. Curinos will move from its current office about two blocks north at 485 Lexington Avenue, though it is unclear when the move will happen. The length of the lease was not disclosed. Asking rents at 10 Grand Central — which is now 98 percent leased, according to the landlord — ranges from $85 to $135 per square foot. Adam Ardise and Rachel Rosenfeld from Cushman & Wakefield represented Curinos in the lease, while JLL’s Mitchell Konsker, Thomas Swartz, Carlee Palmer and Margaux Kelleher represented Marx Realty. Spokespeople for C&W and JLL did not immediately respond to CO’s requests for comment. “The robust leasing activity at 10 Grand Central demonstrates the ongoing appeal of thoughtfully designed workplaces and white glove service that combine to give companies an elevated office experience,” Craig Deitelzweig, CEO of Marx Realty, said in a statement. “Curinos’ decision to relocate within the Grand Central market speaks to the differentiated hospitality-infused experience we’ve created at 10 Grand Central and further strengthens the building’s exceptional tenant roster,” Deitelzweig added. 10 Grand Central is a hospitality-infused office building that offers tenants a variety of amenities, including a reservable house car for transportation, a lounge and cafe on the seventh floor, a 1930s French garden-style outdoor terrace, and a newly opened speakeasy-style restaurant called Highball Ltd. Other tenants at 10 Grand Central include data center developer Edged, bank holding company Merchants Bancorp and research firm Green Street Advisors. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Curinos (fintech data firm) leases 14k sf at Marx Realty's 10 Grand Central, Manhattan office.
Building now 98% leased at $85–$135/sf; tenant relocating from nearby 485 Lexington Avenue.
Fintech and financial services leasing activity signals recovery in trophy Midtown office markets.
stories.summary: Curinos, a global data intelligence firm serving the financial services industry, signed a 14,000-sf lease at Marx Realty's 10 Grand Central in Manhattan after relocating from nearby 485 Lexington Avenue. The building is now 98% leased with asking rents of $85–$135 per square foot. The transaction highlights renewed leasing momentum in thoughtfully renovated Midtown trophy office buildings with premium service.
stories.impact: Fintech and financial services tenants are returning to high-amenity Midtown Manhattan office; 98% occupancy and $85–$135/sf rents suggest stabilization in trophy class-A after post-pandemic softness. Developers should monitor financial services cluster recovery in Midtown—proximity to Wall Street and major banking hubs underpins resilience, creating demand for residential near financial jobs (workforce housing in Hudson Yards, Midtown, and adjacent neighborhoods).

stories.title: Iris Escarrá of Greenberg Traurig: 5 Questions
stories.excerpt: As co-chair of the land use practice at law firm Greenberg Traurig, Iris Escarrá has worked on a number of high-profile projects. In one recent example, Escarrá was part of the team that won approvals for Miami Freedom Park, the mixed-use development near Miami International Airport that includes Nu Stadium, the 26,700-seat home of Major League Soccer’s Inter Miami CF. Before the stadium opened in April, she led the team that guided the development team through environmental remediation, code compliance and traffic and aviation issues. The following conversation has been edited for length and clarity.   Commercial Observer: Now that Nu Stadium is open and Inter Miami is playing matches there, what lessons did you learn from the project? Iris Escarrá: There’s so many. Some of them I have to cover over drinks. Some of them I can cover here. The stadium is actually one component. It’s 12 acres of a 73-acre complex that’s mixed-use. So while the stadium is the biggest to come in, we have retail coming in, hotel, office, and so forth that we’re also working on. It’s very exciting because that’s what’s actually going to create the whole district. Inter Miami has 17 home games, and they’ll have other events there, concerts, soccer games for high schools, but it’s never going to be a 365-day-a-year building. So it’s really the other uses around it that are going to create the district. And those uses around it are actually what helps with some of the traffic items because the restaurants and the retail are open a few hours before the match, so if you want to go and have lunch before the game, you could do that. If you want to go after the game, go hang out. So what happens is that the mixed-use district around the stadiums is what’s really creating the whole district. When are those other uses going to be completed? We have a few buildings that are in for permitting now, some food and beverage, some entertainment. T
bullets: Iris Escarrá (Greenberg Traurig) led approvals for Miami Freedom Park, 73-acre mixed-use near MIA airport.
Nu Stadium (26.7k-seat Inter Miami CF home) opened April 2024; retail, hotel, office phases ongoing.
Complex permitting navigated environmental remediation, aviation, traffic, code compliance.
stories.summary: Iris Escarrá, co-chair of the land-use practice at Greenberg Traurig, led the legal team securing approvals and navigating complex environmental and aviation permitting for Miami Freedom Park, a 73-acre mixed-use development anchored by Nu Stadium, the 26,700-seat home of MLS team Inter Miami CF. The stadium opened in April 2024; the broader complex will include retail, hotel, office, and additional residential phases. The project required environmental remediation, code compliance, and coordination with Miami International Airport operations.
stories.impact: Major sports stadiums and anchored mixed-use projects drive neighborhood formation and long-term residential demand near airports/employment hubs. Miami Freedom Park's phased development (hotel, office, retail, and residential components) will drive population and job agglomeration; developers should acquire land near stadium-catalyzed mixed-use precincts—capture early-stage renter demand before land values fully capitalize stadium impact.

stories.title: Hochul taps developers to build $1B Hell’s Kitchen towers
stories.excerpt: The state has tapped three New York developers to build two towers with more than 1,100 homes on a publicly owned parking lot in Hell’s Kitchen across from the USS Intrepid museum. Gov. Kathy Hochul selected The Gotham Organization, Fisher Brothers and Mural Real Estate to construct the highrises with 1,127 condos and apartments at 621 West 45th Street, Crain’s New York reported.  Plans for the $1 billion project, dubbed Hudson Landing, include two connected towers containing 1.3 million square feet on a 1.2-acre lot across from Pier 86 on the Hudson River. Of the 1,127 units, 108 would be […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Hochul taps Gotham Organization, Fisher Brothers, Mural Real Estate to build Hudson Landing, 1.127k units on state parking lot.
621 West 45th St, Hell's Kitchen: 108 for-sale, 338 affordable rentals (40%–130% AMI), $1B mixed-use, 1.3M sf.
State overrides zoning (light manufacturing); RUSH policy permits mixed-income housing on underutilized public land.
stories.summary: Governor Kathy Hochul selected Gotham Organization, Fisher Brothers, and Mural Real Estate to develop Hudson Landing, a $1 billion mixed-use project on a 1.2-acre state-owned parking lot at 621 West 45th Street in Hell's Kitchen. The two connected towers will contain 1,127 units (108 for-sale condos, 338 affordable rentals for 40%–130% AMI) across 1.3 million square feet, plus ~10k sf of retail/restaurants and a public park. The state will sell the site at fair market value and override local zoning (currently light manufacturing) under the Redeveloping Underused Sites as Housing (RUSH) initiative launched in 2024.
stories.impact: State-backed, zoning-override programs (RUSH) are fast-tracking infill development in Manhattan and reducing entitlement risk for mixed-income housing. Hudson Landing's 338 affordable units (40%–130% AMI) and institutional developer lineup signal reliable capital for workforce/middle-income housing. Developers should monitor RUSH pipeline for additional public-land opportunities in high-value districts—regulatory certainty and state equity participation lower cost of capital and shorten entitlement timelines.

stories.title: Hochul Selects Developers to Build 1,127 Housing Units in Hell’s Kitchen
stories.excerpt: A parking lot near the Intrepid Museum along the Hell’s Kitchen waterfront is about to become housing. Developers Gotham Organization, Fisher Brothers and Mural Real Estate Group have been chosen by Gov. Kathy Hochul to transform the 50,584-square-foot parking lot at 621 West 45th Street into two residential towers containing about 1,127 new homes, the governor’s office announced Wednesday. Crain’s New York Business first reported the news. Hochul announced in February of last year that she wanted to turn the state-owned parking lot along the West Side Highway — which is currently being used by the Intrepid Museum — into a residential development to help dent the city’s housing shortage. “The West Side of Manhattan has a storied history as a vibrant, inclusive community, and I’m committed to strengthening that legacy by building for a more affordable future,” Hochul said in the 2025 announcement. “By transforming this underutilized state-owned property into a dynamic mixed-use development with a significant affordable housing component, we’re taking aim at low housing supply in a high-demand area and building a stronger community for residents in Hell’s Kitchen and beyond.” Gotham, Fisher and Mural will redevelop the lot into two connected residential towers that will feature 1,127 units, with some 338 being set aside as affordable housing for those earning between 40 percent and 130 percent of the area median income, Crain’s reported. The total will include 108 for-sale condo units, with some 28 set aside as affordable housing. “Gotham has spent decades partnering with government, community organizations, and mission-driven institutions on the West Side of Manhattan to deliver housing that serves New Yorkers across the income spectrum, and this project builds on that legacy,” David Picket, CEO of Gotham, said in a statement to Commercial Observer. “This transformative development will deliver hundreds of permanently affordable homes, including homes for
bullets: Gotham Organization, Fisher Brothers, Mural selected to build 1.127k units at 621 W 45th St, Hell's Kitchen.
Two towers on 50.5k-sf state parking lot; 108 for-sale, 338 affordable rentals, $1B development.
Hochul RUSH policy enables zoning override; site at former gas plant near Intrepid Museum waterfront.
stories.summary: Governor Hochul announced the selection of Gotham Organization, Fisher Brothers, and Mural Real Estate Group to transform a 50,584-sf state-owned parking lot at 621 West 45th Street into two residential towers containing approximately 1,127 new homes. The project, Hudson Landing, will include mixed-income housing (condos and rentals), retail, dining, and a public park on the Hell's Kitchen waterfront near the Intrepid Museum. The state is exercising its Redeveloping Underused Sites as Housing (RUSH) authority to override local zoning and accelerate development.
stories.impact: Duplicate/similar story to idx 9; state zoning override and public-land sales pipelines lower acquisition and entitlement risk. RUSH initiative removing friction from mixed-income housing on underutilized public sites; developers should track state and municipal inventories of underused parking lots and public buildings in high-value urban cores—regulatory certainty and state partnerships unlock capital-efficient mixed-income development.

stories.title: Jewish High School Championed by Jeff Sutton Secures $30M in Construction Financing
stories.excerpt: A Jewish school in Brooklyn’s Gravesend neighborhood has received approval from the state attorney general for a $30 million construction loan, Commercial Observer has learned. Magen David Yeshivah, which operates multiple day schools in Gravesend, secured the $30 million loan from BankUnited for the continued construction of a new Sephardic high school along McDonald Avenue. The floating-rate, three-year loan agreement mortgages Magen David’s nearby elementary school property at 2130 McDonald Avenue. Religious corporations in New York must have approval from the state attorney general or a supreme court justice before mortgaging, selling or leasing most real estate. The school acquired its future home across three sites — 1905, 1911 and 1915 McDonald Avenue — in 2023 for $13.5 million. Jeff Sutton, a prominent retail landlord and an active Magen David trustee, assisted the school in the deal, The Real Deal reported at the time. It is unclear whether Sutton, or any brokers, lent their expertise in arranging the new loan terms. A diagram of a planned school at 370 Quentin Road, Brooklyn. DIAGRAM: Sherida Paulsen Architect via NYC Department of Buildings Plans for the $50 million high school span 81,000 square feet, PincusCo first reported. Remaining construction costs total $37.46 million, and the building is slated for completion in early 2027. Magen David school, founded in 1946, sold its former high school at 7801 Bay Parkway to Success Academy Charter Schools for $37.2 million in 2024, according to TRD. It continues to operate an early childhood center at 2170 McDonald Avenue and its nearby elementary school. Spokespeople for Magen David and BankUnited did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Magen David Yeshivah secures $30M construction loan from BankUnited for Sephardic high school in Brooklyn Gravesend.
Floating-rate, three-year loan; school acquired three sites in 2023 for $13.5M; Jeff Sutton (retail landlord) is trustee.
Educational real estate financing; modest transaction size signals institutional interest in faith-based/school development.
stories.summary: Magen David Yeshivah, a Brooklyn-based Jewish school network operating multiple day schools in Gravesend, secured a $30 million construction loan from BankUnited for a new Sephardic high school on McDonald Avenue. The floating-rate, three-year facility is secured by a mortgage on the school's nearby elementary property. The school had acquired its future high-school site across three parcels in 2023 for $13.5 million, with retail landlord Jeff Sutton (an active trustee) facilitating the transaction.
stories.impact: Modest educational real estate financing; limited direct impact on multifamily development. However, faith-based institutional development in Brooklyn underscores land scarcity and valuation inflation in neighborhood cores (Gravesend). Monitor similar institutional conversions and land-banking in dense outer-borough neighborhoods—may signal emerging residential demand as schools and nonprofits develop campuses and drive foot traffic, housing, and service-sector employment.

stories.title: PTM Partners, Peacock launch subsidy-free workforce housing platform
stories.excerpt: Florida-based PTM Partners and Peacock Capital launched Inception Housing, a platform designed to deliver lower-cost housing without relying on government aid.  The venture targets middle-income renters earning between 80 and 120 percent of the area median income, cutting development costs by handling much of the design, construction and material procurement in-house, according to a news release.  By using a standardized building design and cold-formed steel purchased directly from suppliers, Inception Housing can cut hard construction costs by about 25 percent compared to similar products, PTM Partners CEO Michael Tillman said. The platform’s strategy delivers buildings in six to seven months, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: PTM Partners and Peacock Capital launch Inception Housing, subsidy-free workforce platform targeting 80%–120% AMI renters.
Cold-formed steel and standardized design cut construction costs ~25%; six-to-seven-month delivery vs. traditional 12–14 months.
First project: Magnolia Flats, South Orlando (88 units, 14 existing + 74 new); South Florida needs 30k+ units.
stories.summary: Florida-based PTM Partners and Peacock Capital launched Inception Housing, a platform delivering subsidy-free workforce housing for renters earning 80–120% of area median income. Using standardized design, cold-formed steel direct procurement, and in-house construction management, Inception cuts hard costs ~25% versus traditional construction and achieves delivery in six to seven months (50% faster). The first project, Magnolia Flats in South Orlando, will add 74 units to an existing 14-townhome site. South Florida faces a 30k+-unit shortage; statewide, Florida needs ~121k units.
stories.impact: Cost-reduction and speed-to-market innovations (standardized design, modular steel, direct procurement) are materializing at scale in supply-constrained Florida. Inception's approach—eliminating government subsidies through hard-cost compression—proves 80–120% AMI workforce housing can pencil without tax credits or LIHTC. Developers should track standardized, modular approaches and cold-formed-steel suppliers; rapid delivery and cost control will become competitive necessity as Florida shortage persists and competing submarkets (Austin, Raleigh) deploy similar playbooks.

stories.title: Dallas apartment pipeline gets a boost with pair of financing deals
stories.excerpt: Two developers of a pair of apartment complexes in the Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex recently secured financing for their separate projects.  Endeavor Real Estate locked in financing for their luxury high-rise at 2700 McKinney Avenue named Lucille. Additionally, StreetLights Residential snagged $54 million in equity financing for an apartment tower in Plano, Texas, which will be built in the redevelopment of The Park at Legacy at 6501 Legacy Drive.  Lucille will feature 265 units across 22 stories in Uptown, according to the Dallas Morning News. The development is helmed by Austin-based Endeavor and equity partner Canyon Partners Real Estate. Construction on […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Endeavor Real Estate and StreetLights Residential secure financing for two DFW apartment towers: Lucille (265 units, Uptown), Park at Legacy redevelopment (Plano).
Lucille: 22-story, 320k sf, ground-floor retail, rooftop pool, Q4 2028 delivery; StreetLights secures $54M equity.
Uptown and Legacy office-to-mixed-use conversions signal sustained demand in DFW metro core submarkets.
stories.summary: Two Dallas-Fort Worth multifamily developers secured financing: Endeavor Real Estate locked in funding for Lucille, a 265-unit luxury high-rise at 2700 McKinney Avenue in Uptown (22 stories, 320k sf, Q4 2028 opening), with equity partner Canyon Partners; StreetLights Residential raised $54 million in equity financing for an apartment tower in Plano as part of the 107-acre Park at Legacy mixed-use redevelopment. Endeavor's Lucille targets working professionals in Uptown's financial district with premium amenities (fitness, rooftop pool, observation deck, dog spa). Construction on Lucille begins summer 2026.
stories.impact: DFW metro is absorbing luxury apartment capital and mixed-use redevelopment financing; Uptown and Legacy Park (office-to-mixed-use conversions) signal sustained downtown densification and workforce attraction. Rising completion of high-rises in these core submarkets will drive above-average rent growth. Developers should prioritize infill and conversion sites near job clusters (Uptown financial services, Legacy Park tech/corporate); class-A rents in central DFW will support dense, mixed-use development ahead of supply constraints tightening further.

stories.title: Texas’ top construction permits for the week ending July 14, 2026
stories.excerpt: New Construction Fort Worth’s public hospital John Peter Smith Hospital filed the state’s biggest construction project of the week to build a $300 million central utility plant at 1575 South Main Street on Fort Worth’s South Side. Construction on the 113,000-square-foot facility started at the end of June and is expected to wrap up in May 2028.  Job filing number: TABS2026024842 Houston Independent School District filed plans to build a 216,000-square-foot career and technical center at 9220 Jutland Road in Houston. Construction on the project, which will feature classrooms, labs, a multipurpose center, kitchen and administration spaces, is expected to […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Amazon files permit for 1.2M-sf distribution center in Terrell TX (DFW suburb); $98M, 12-month build starting Aug 2026.
Fort Worth: $300M central utility plant at John Peter Smith Hospital (113k sf, started June 2026, ends May 2028).
Houston ISD and City of Dallas also filing major permits; sustained permit velocity in Texas construction.
stories.summary: Texas filed multiple major construction permits in the week ending July 14, 2026: Amazon is building a $98 million, 1.2 million-sf distribution center in Terrell (DFW suburb), with 12-month construction starting August 2026; Fort Worth's public hospital filed a $300 million, 113k-sf central utility plant (June 2026–May 2028); Houston ISD is building a 216k-sf career and technical education center; Dallas Museum of Art is upgrading mechanical and fire systems ($20M). Additional permits include tech tenant improvements and industrial projects across Texas.
stories.impact: Amazon logistics expansion in DFW suburbs (Terrell, Dallas-area) drives long-haul employment and warehouse/industrial logistics demand. Major public infrastructure and education projects signal sustained Texas in-migration and institutional capital confidence. Developers should monitor Amazon and last-mile distribution buildout across DFW, Houston, Austin—each new distribution center anchors surrounding workforce housing demand in suburban corridors. Workforce housing near Amazon facilities (sub-$1,200 rents, 30–45 min commute radius) remains undersupplied across Texas metros.

stories.title: Dolphins out, superyachts in: David Martin’s marina plan at former Seaquarium getting underway
stories.excerpt: The site of the former Miami Seaquarium on Virginia Key is headed for a major redevelopment.  Miami-Dade County and Terra Group’s David Martin want to trade dolphin shows for superyachts with an application to build what could be the largest marina in the county, the Miami Herald reported.  The application, submitted to state regulators, calls for 325 boat slips, about 90 of which could accommodate yachts longer than 80 feet, and a 500-boat dry stack, the outlet said. The plan includes a fuel depot, a network of floating docks that extends halfway across the channel separating Virginia Key from nearby […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Terra Group (David Martin) negotiating ground lease with Miami-Dade for former Miami Seaquarium redevelopment into superyacht marina.
Application: 325 boat slips (90+ for yachts >80 ft), 500-boat dry stack, 19k-sf fuel depot, wave-break fishing pier.
Florida DEP concerns on wetlands, dredging, coral, water quality; Martin receives 95% of marina slip profit.
stories.summary: Terra Group's David Martin is negotiating a ground lease with Miami-Dade County to redevelop the former Miami Seaquarium site (4400 Rickenbacker Causeway) into a superyacht marina and ancillary facilities. The application to state regulators calls for 325 boat slips (including ~90 for 80+ ft yachts), a 500-boat dry stack, fuel depot, floating docks, and a wave-break fishing pier. Martin's operator agreement grants him 95% of slip revenue. The county evicted the Seaquarium in 2024 over animal welfare violations. The Florida Department of Environmental Protection is requesting details on dredging impacts, wetlands management, and design alternatives; the lease negotiations remain ongoing.
stories.impact: Superyacht and marine infrastructure development on reclaimed public land is a small but capital-intensive use that displaces mixed-income housing opportunity. Virginia Key waterfront redevelopment into marina-only use does not generate residential demand or workforce housing supply. However, if Marina redevelopment includes hotel, restaurant, retail, or office components, it will drive long-term service-sector employment and waterfront neighborhood growth. Monitor companion mixed-use plans—Miami waterfront scarcity demands mixed-use intensity. If purely marine/marina, limited multifamily impact.

stories.title: TIF funding for the 78 gets key approval for $425M for needed infrastructure
stories.excerpt: The Chicago City Council Committee on Finance approved a deal to fund $425 million of infrastructure at the 78, a mixed-use development that will include a privately-funded new stadium for the Major League Soccer franchise the Chicago Fire.  The deal would repay developer Related Midwest for project-supporting structures using tax increment financing money, including $287 million in TIF money from a nearby district. The next step in the deal will be getting the formal green light from the City Council, who are set to hold a final vote on the agreement on Wednesday, according to Crain’s.  Joe Mansueto, the Fire’s […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Chicago City Council Finance Committee approves $425M infrastructure funding for The 78 mixed-use (related Midwest, MLS stadium).
TIF deal redirects $287M from Canal/Congress TIF district to Roosevelt/Clark TIF (where The 78 is sited).
Related Midwest receives TIF repayment for infrastructure; Joe Mansueto's Fire stadium privately funded, public support for roads/utilities.
stories.summary: The Chicago City Council Finance Committee approved a $425 million infrastructure funding deal for The 78, a mixed-use development by Related Midwest that will include a privately funded stadium for MLS franchise the Chicago Fire. The structure redirects $287 million in tax increment financing from the nearby Canal/Congress TIF district into the Roosevelt/Clark TIF district where The 78 is planned. Related Midwest will be reimbursed for project-supporting infrastructure (roads, utilities). The full City Council is scheduled to vote on the deal Wednesday. Some aldermen oppose the TIF transfer, citing future public-transit funding conflicts.
stories.impact: TIF-funded infrastructure accelerates public investment in South Loop/Roosevelt district, reducing developer friction and speeding mixed-use entitlements. Related Midwest's $1B+ mixed-use stadium-anchored project will drive residential demand and neighborhood densification in South Loop. However, TIF reallocation away from transit (Canal/Congress district) may slow future public-transportation improvements. Developers should monitor competing TIF allocations and transit timing—South Loop will absorb significant new multifamily demand from The 78 and Fire stadium jobs, but transit capacity limitations may constrain long-term rent growth. Early-stage acquisition near The 78 and Blue Line stations offers upside.
